ground_truth.title: 7 Major Diet Trends for 2025, According to Nutritionists
ground_truth.author: Korin Miller
ground_truth.main_content:
Food and nutrition advice is constantly changing, and each year sees certain themes rise in popularity. This year is no exception. There are already diet trends emerging in 2025, and nutritionists say they're largely positive—and good for you. Expect protein to continue to be an 'it' macronutrient, along with a growing movement away from alcohol. Plenty of people are also focused on being the healthiest version of themselves, and that can have a big impact on where we're headed with dietary trends.

PROTEIN EVERYTHING: Expect protein's role as a buzzy macronutrient to continue, especially as more people start taking weight loss medications. Protein remains a priority, with consumers focusing on its role in muscle maintenance, weight management, and overall health. But innovative sources of protein like lab-grown meat and plant-based proteins are gaining more traction as people focus more on sustainability and health. The conversation is also shifting toward quality over quantity, with people seeking proteins that support gut health and align with personalized dietary needs.

SIMPLE NON-ALCOHOLIC DRINKS: Research shows that more people view alcohol as unhealthy—and that's causing fewer to drink it these days, especially younger generations. Mocktails may not maintain their momentum as a mainstream trend. Instead, people may pivot toward simpler alternatives like sparkling water, functional teas, or beverages with added benefits like electrolytes or adaptogens. Momentum around drinks seems to favor convenience and practicality over the crafted complexity of mocktails.

A BIG EMPHASIS ON BEANS: Beans aren't a staple food for most Americans, but a panel of top dietitians is hoping to change that. A new report from an advisory committee to the United States Department of Agriculture recommends tweaking the Dietary Guidelines for Americans to suggest that people have at least 2.5 cups of beans and lentils a week. The committee also recommends replacing red and processed meats with beans and suggests thinking of legumes as your primary source of protein.

PERSONALIZED NUTRITION: Those who can afford it are more likely to seek out personalized nutrition this year. Tailored dietary plans based on genetic testing, microbiome analysis, and wearable technology are becoming more accessible. This technology allows individuals to optimize health outcomes by targeting specific needs, from improving sleep quality to managing chronic conditions, making personalized nutrition one of the most exciting trends of 2025.

A MORE BALANCED VIEW OF PROCESSED FOODS: The idea of 'clean eating' will continue to be a focus in 2025. While clean eating often means avoiding ultra-processed foods, there is a growing movement in the dietary community to view food processing on a spectrum instead of just labeling foods as processed or not. Processed foods are, and can be, an integral part of a healthy diet.

BIOHACKING: Biohacking means making small changes to your behavior and health habits to try to improve your health. There seems to be a movement toward more biohacking, with a growing number of people using high-dose vitamins and specialty supplements. It's best to consult with a healthcare provider before putting yourself on a supplement, especially one at high doses.

A NATURAL APPROACH TO HEALTH: While some people are focused on biohacking, there's also a counter-movement that embraces a more natural approach to health. This emphasizes eating whole foods, doing more classic strength training, and getting plenty of outdoor activity. This return to simplicity focuses on maintaining a lean, functional body without over-reliance on modern technology or supplementation.
with: Food and nutrition advice is constantly changing, and each year sees certain themes rise in popularity.
Expect protein to continue to be an 'it' macronutrient, along with a growing movement away from alcohol.
Beans aren't a staple food for most Americans, but a panel of top dietitians is hoping to change that.
Tailored dietary plans based on genetic testing, microbiome analysis, and wearable technology are becoming more accessible.
Processed foods are, and can be, an integral part of a healthy diet.
